names.scientific_name: Cera alba (white beeswax), Cera flava (yellow beeswax)

natural_occurrence.description: A natural wax produced by honeybees to build honeycomb. Composed mainly of esters, fatty acids, and hydrocarbons. White beeswax is bleached; yellow beeswax is unbleached.

manufacturing.description: Harvested from honeycomb, melted, filtered, and cooled. White beeswax is further bleached using hydrogen peroxide or sunlight. Yellow beeswax retains natural carotenoid pigments.

other_uses.cosmetics: Lip balm, moisturizers, makeup, hair pomade
other_uses.industrial: Furniture polish, car wax, candles, leather conditioner
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Ointment base, pill coating, dental impressions
other_uses.household: Candle making, wood finishing, leather treatment

eu.conditions: Authorized as glazing agent for surface treatment

usa.notes: Approved for use as direct food substance

canada.reference: Lists of Permitted Glazing and Polishing Agents

safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: Not specified (no safety concern at current use levels)

regulatory_history.event: Beeswax used for thousands of years in food preservation and coating
EFSA confirms safety of beeswax as food additive

adi_context.source_note: No ADI established - beeswax is considered safe at all levels of consumption due to its inert nature and lack of absorption.

fun_facts: Honeybees produce beeswax from special glands on their abdomen
The same wax used in candy coating is also used in furniture polish and car wax
Beeswax candles were used in churches for over 1,000 years
It takes bees consuming 6-8 pounds of honey to produce 1 pound of beeswax
Completely edible and passes through the digestive system unchanged
Not suitable for vegans as it's an animal product
